phenomenon: coherence between affective concepts and embodied reactions
constructs: embodiment, fluency, epistemic validation, processing efficiency
claim: Alignment between mental affect and bodily state enables smooth information processing by resolving uncertainty about affective appropriateness.

phenomenon: processing scope determines categorical inclusion versus exclusion
constructs: global-local processing, construal scope, category boundaries, assimilation-contrast
claim: Broad attentional scope promotes seeing targets as category members (assimilation), while narrow scope promotes seeing them as distinct (contrast).

phenomenon: emotion communicates limits but structural context determines effect direction
constructs: information distribution, power asymmetry, fear of rejection, deception, limit inference
claim: Anger's bargaining advantage reverses when opponents can deceive to lower perceived limits or when rejection carries low costs.

constructs: egocentric bias, assumed similarity, relationship confidence, communal motivation
claim: Caring individuals assume partners reciprocate care through egocentric projection, enabling relationship investment despite ambiguous behavioral evidence.

phenomenon: external system endorsement compensates for lost personal control
constructs: personal control, system benevolence, order-seeking motive, secondary control
claim: When personal control is threatened, people increase reliance on external controlling systems (God, government) as substitutable means of maintaining perceived order.

phenomenon: moral rebel creates implicit reproach threatening onlooker adequacy
constructs: imagined rejection, moral self-concept, implicit comparison, self-threat
claim: Those who failed to rebel resent rebels because their principled stance implicitly condemns non-rebellion, creating a threat to moral self-worth that defensive rejection mitigates.
